title: III: Evaluation of library quality
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1jv26","text":"Here we outline analytic approaches used to evaluate quality of PhIP-Seq libraries following construction. For PanSeq libraries we estimate peptide tile representation in the final library, uniformness of coverage and error profiles. In addition, by comparing QC metrics for PanSeq libraries at different stages of preparation, we identify several factors that affect library quality.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Several factors should be evaluated to ensure adequate quality of the physical libraries prior to use and for purposes of subsequent analysis. One of the primary quality control assessments is the estimation of the proportion of tiles in the in silico design that are detectable in the physical library. Therefore, we sequenced the PanSeq synthetic oligonucleotide pool (“oligo”), the original unexpanded packaged phage library (“Rd0”), and two production lots of phage library used for immunoprecipitation ("lot_2" and "lot_3"). The oligonucleotide pool and Rd0 library were sequenced as separate P1-P4 libraries corresponding to synthetic oligo pools. These pools were sequenced to 85x-119x depth for the oligo pools and 46x-123x depth for Rd0 pools. Lot_2 and lot_3, packaged library pools that were independently derived from Rd0 pool, were sequenced to 475x and 294x depth for lot_2 and lot_3, respectively. When we plot the distribution of per-tile coverage, we find that nearly all tiles can be detected in the output, but that the distribution of per-tile coverage is broader in Rd0 and lots 2 and 3 compared to the starting oligo library ( Figure 1 ). However, the distribution is similar between independently derived lot_2 and lot_3. Figure 1. The distribution of per-tile coverage of the starting oligo pool, Rd0 phage library, and production lots 2 and 3. For comparison, the distribution of tile coverage in phage libraries (golden) is plotted together with the starting oligo pool (magenta). We then computed the proportion of individual tiles that were not detected in each experiment and the breadth of coverage (proportion of all tiles within 10-fold from mean coverage) ( Figure 2) . Overall, we find that at least 96.5% of all peptides were successfully cloned into the Rd0 library, and we detect >95.4% of all tiles in production lots 2 and 3. Figure 2. The proportion of undetected peptide tiles in each of the libraries ( left ), the breadth of coverage depth distributions ( middle ), and upper and lower quantiles for coverage distribution ( right ). Breadth of coverage was defined as the proportion of the library within 10-fold of the mean coverage. 5th and 95th percentiles were calculated for the log10 fold change from the mean plot ( Figure 1 ). Detailed examination of the breadth of coverage distribution profiles shows that while the tile representation becomes notably less even in production lots 2 and 3, more than 69.5% of all tiles have coverage within 10-fold of the mean, and for >90% of tiles, the difference in coverage is less than 1000-fold. Further, a comparison of the number of undetected tiles between oligo, Rd0, and production lots 2 and 3 ( Figure 2 ) indicates that some tiles detected in the oligo pool sequencing output were not detected in the packaged phage libraries. Figure 3. Upset plot of the number of peptide tiles not detected in each of the libraries and their combinations. Groups with a small number of elements (N<150) and the largest group of peptides detected in all of the libraries (N=869382, 92.2% of all peptide tiles) are excluded from the plot. Multiple factors can affect the detection of individual tiles in the final library. These include statistical sampling, PCR amplification biases, and a peptide's effect on the growth of a given phage during the amplification stage. To parse potential factors affecting apparent peptide tile loss in PhIP-Seq libraries, we evaluated whether tiles that were not detected in the oligo pool or one of the packaged libraries were also undetected in other libraries ( Figure 3 ). The upset plot clearly indicates that a major proportion of observed losses are due to sampling and observed in only one of the samples. In contrast, only 0.87% (N=8,191) of all peptide tiles were not detected in any of the packaged libraries. To evaluate potential GC bias and subsequent suppression of PCR amplification as a potential driver of tile loss in packaged libraries, we parsed all of the tiles based on their GC content and plotted their relative representation in the amplified oligo pool and production lot_2 library ( Figure 4 ). Indeed, while tile coverage is not strongly dependent on GC content in the oligo pool, we find a notable effect of GC content on tile representation in lot_2 packaged library (results for other packaged libraries are similar), with both extremes under-represented, and nearly no sequences exceeding 73-75% GC ( Figure 4 ). Figure 4. Dependence of relative peptide coverage on GC content of oligonucleotide However, GC content does not fully explain which sequences were lost during phage packaging and library preparation. Specifically, while those sequences lost in the oligo pool and all of the phage libraries (Rd0, lot_2 and 3, N=504, see Figure 3 ) have strongly biased to extremes GC content ( Figure 5 ), those sequences detected in the oligo pool but absent from Rd0, lot_2 and lot_3 (N=7,687) have a GC content distribution largely similar to composition of all tiles in library ( Figure 5 ). Thus, other factors also affect the representation of individual T7 clones in the final packaged library. Figure 5. Most phages depleted in Rd0 pool have ~50% GC content Another factor affecting PhIP-seq performance is the fidelity of oligonucleotide synthesis and subsequent library preparation steps. These steps affect the proportion of T7 clones that faithfully encode the intended oligonucleotide sequence. We thus estimated error rate and per-oligo indel (insertion/deletion) proportion in PanSeq libraries ( Figure 6 ). We find that both the PanSeq per-base substitution rate is <<1% and that PanSeq libraries are largely devoid of insertions and deletions. Furthermore, a majority of PanSeq libraries consist of full-length, non-rearranged sequences. Taken together, we estimated that the majority of library peptides are error-free. Figure 6. Proportion of substitutions and indels per oligo in PanSeq pools and proportion of full-length tiles.
creator: Pavel Khil
